definition: From G2126 (eulabes); properly, caution, i.e. (religiously) reverence, piety; godly fear.

semantic_categories.name: Reverent caution before God
semantic_categories.description: In Hebrews 5:7, Christ's prayers during His earthly life were heard 'for his godly fear' (KJV: 'in that he feared'). The noun conveys not terror but the careful, reverent disposition that takes God seriously — a well-placed caution (eu + labein, 'to take well') that shapes how one approaches the divine. Christ's eulabeia was the reverential posture underlying His submission to the Father's will.

semantic_categories.name: Worship disposition accompanying grace
semantic_categories.description: In Hebrews 12:28, believers receiving an unshakable kingdom are told to 'serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear.' Here eulabeia is paired with aidos (G127, 'reverence/modesty'), forming a compound expression for the appropriate worship disposition. Grace does not eliminate reverent caution; it grounds it. The worshipper who understands what God has done responds not with casual familiarity but with awe-infused gratitude.

collocations.pattern: paired in Heb 12:28 — 'reverence and godly fear'
eulabeia as manner of acceptable worship (Heb 12:28)

synonyms.distinction: General fear/terror (47 occ); can be negative (fright) or positive (reverence). Eulabeia is always positive — caution arising from proper regard, never raw terror.
Godliness/piety as habitual conduct (15 occ); eulabeia is the internal disposition (reverent caution) whereas eusebeia is the outward pattern of devout living.
Shame-based modesty/reverence (2 occ); paired with eulabeia in Heb 12:28. Aidos is self-restraint from a sense of propriety; eulabeia is caution from a sense of God's greatness.

word_family.distinction: Verb form (2 occ): 'to act with reverent caution.' Used of Noah 'moved with fear' (Heb 11:7) and of Paul's rescuers 'fearing' (Acts 23:10).
Adjective form (3 occ): 'devout, cautious.' Used of Simeon (Luke 2:25), Jews at Pentecost (Acts 2:5), and those who buried Stephen (Acts 8:2).
